title: Circuits of Quantum Hashing and Quantum Fourier Transform for a Cactus as a Qubit Connectivity Graph
abstract: We present a quantum circuit implementation of the quantum hashing algorithm (quantum fingerprinting) for a quantum device with restrictions on the application of two-qubit gates by a qubit connectivity graph. We present an optimization technique for the shallow circuit for quantum hashing in the case of a cactus as a qubit connectivity graph. The algorithm has $O(n^3)$ complexity to build the circuit, where $n$ is the number of qubits and $m$ is the number of connections (edges) in the graph. It is improvement compared to the existing exponential-time algorithm in the case of arbitrary graphs. The algorithm uses solution for the shortest non-simple 1-covering path problem as a subroutine. We present an $O(n^3)$-time solution for this graph-theory problem in the case of a cactus. This result can be interesting independently. The algorithm also used for improving of the quantum circuit for Quantum Fourier Transform.
summary: This paper provides an efficient, polynomial-time method for building optimized quantum circuits for Quantum Hashing and QFT on cactus-structured quantum hardware. By solving a specific graph-theoretic path problem, the authors significantly reduce the number of CNOT gates required compared to previous methods.
main_problem: Optimizing quantum circuit construction, specifically minimizing CNOT gate counts for Quantum Hashing and Quantum Fourier Transform (QFT), under restricted qubit connectivity constraints.
main_result: The authors present an O(n^3) algorithm to construct shallow circuits for cactus graph architectures, significantly reducing CNOT complexity compared to exponential-time algorithms for arbitrary graphs.
method: The approach uses a solution to the 'Shortest non-simple 1-covering path' (1-SNSCPP) problem via dynamic programming on a tree-transformed cactus graph to guide qubit movement via SWAP gates.
model_or_system: A quantum device where two-qubit gates are restricted by a 'cactus' connectivity graph, where any two simple cycles share at most one vertex.
key_observables: CNOT gate count (cost) and circuit depth.
important_parameters: Number of qubits (n), number of edges (m), path length (k), and the length of the input word (l).
main_assumptions: The optimization results are specifically tailored to the cactus graph topology and assume the ability to implement SWAP and controlled rotation gates.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate shallow circuit architectures, gate decompositions (CRy, SWAP, CRd), comparisons between standard and optimized paths on cactus graphs, and the construction of auxiliary trees for the DP algorithm.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of connectivity-constrained circuit optimization, defines the cactus graph model, presents a polynomial-time solution for the 1-SNSCPP problem using dynamic programming, applies this to Quantum Hashing and QFT, and provides complexity analysis and CNOT cost bounds.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Fig. 2. Representation of CRy, SWAP gates, and a pair CRy and SWAP gates using only basic gates.
